steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: A permanent magnet…
options: Only attracts metals when placed near another magnet
Produces a magnetic field at all times without needing a current
Can be switched off by reversing the current through it
Only works when heated above room temperature
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! A permanent magnet always produces its own magnetic field — it does not need a current or another magnet nearby. Temporary (induced) magnets only behave like magnets when placed in an external magnetic field.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! A permanent magnet produces a magnetic field all the time. Temporary magnets — like soft iron — only become magnetised when placed inside an external field.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA Physics specification 4.7.1 — Poles of a magnet

steps.title: Permanent and Temporary Magnets
steps.bodyHTML: Permanent magnets produce their own magnetic field at all times and cannot easily be demagnetised. Temporary (induced) magnets, such as soft iron, only become magnetised when placed inside an external magnetic field and lose their magnetism when removed. Like poles repel and unlike poles attract — a north pole always repels another north pole but attracts a south pole.

relationships: Induced magnet → magnetised only in external field

commonErrors: Thinking a permanent magnet needs a current to work
Forgetting that like poles repel (not attract)

aqa.questionText: Describe the difference between a permanent magnet and a temporary (induced) magnet.

steps.label: Define permanent magnet
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — produces a magnetic field at all times / does not need an external field

steps.label: Define temporary magnet
steps.content: State what makes it temporary: a temporary magnet only becomes magnetised when placed in an external magnetic field and loses its magnetism when removed from that field.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — only magnetised in external field / loses magnetism when field removed

aqa.finalAnswer: Permanent magnets always produce a field; temporary magnets only become magnetised inside an external field.

aqa.questionText: Two bar magnets are placed with their north poles facing each other. What happens?
aqa.commonMistake: Option A is a trap — unlike poles attract, but like poles repel.
